column2: — What are you doing tomorrow?
— Do you need help?
— he texted back.
Miyechka had disliked falling in love ever since her youth.
All these attractions were burdensome.
As soon as she felt that someone liked her, she would immediately vanish, flee and shut herself off.
— Sooner or later someone will take your fortress by storm, — Maria would say, while she herself fretted and would tell her mother: 'Mom, Miyechka will probably never know love.'
Miyechka had met no small number of men who made her heart beat a little faster.
Students, colleagues and even long-forgotten crushes from her youth fell in love with her, and she fled from them as from the plague.
There was nothing fatally attractive about her, but her overall image would sometimes hit something in men, and they would remain, for a long while, somewhat beside themselves.
They went about like wounded game, steering clear of the hunting trails.
If before this had frightened Miyechka, now, as a grown woman, there was something in it she liked.
She felt a kind of forbidden pleasure at being able, so inadvertently, to hit a living creature.
Because, unlike Lilichka, who always fell as the first victim and turned her life upside down every time a fatal love came her way, Miyechka looked on it all skeptically.
— How can you be like that?
— Lilichka once said to her after yet another upheaval in her life.
— It's as if you skim off the top everything that brings only pleasure, and never dig your way down to the suffering.
— That's logical, isn't it?
— Miyechka would ask.
— But it's unfair!
— Lilichka shouted.
— You get the buzz without the morning nausea, you just savor the wine, and you never get a headache, your mood never sours afterward… It's so unfair.
Miyechka would shrug.
— But I always have to reach the bitter part.
Always dig my way down to the suffering.
It's never once happened that I simply got the pleasure.
But there was no escaping fairness.
Someone did hit Miyechka after all, and she too dug her way down to suffering.
Her breath caught, and she wanted to turn her life upside down.
The remnants of her good sense helped her take a pause, right up until the roof started leaking.
— I'll come, — he wrote.
Miyechka went back to her room.
Looked in the mirror.
She took the scissors, lightly trimmed a strand that stuck out somewhere from behind her ear.
She combed her hair, which in a fair wave almost reached her shoulders, turned out the whole suitcase of clothes and tried on half of them.
Everything was wrong.
She went outside.
Lusya came running toward her, saying: 'Mia!
Mia!'
Behind her stood Marta, laughing.
— Our girl has started talking!
— Maria cried out.
— Oh, my little bird!
— Mia scooped the child up into her arms.
— Mia!
— the girl cried.
— Lusya!
— exclaimed Miyechka.
— Tomorrow they'll fix our roof for us!
— Did you find someone?
— the grandmother asked again, leaning on her cane.
— Did someone take the sin upon his soul?
— He did, — Lusya pulled Mia by the hair, and Mia pried open the small, tenacious little fingers.
— One militant atheist turned up.
Miyechka drove out in the morning to fetch the atheist, to meet him at the turn off the highway.
For half the evening, so that no one would see, she had once more been trying on what to wear.
And finally she found her jeans, the ones she'd worn back as a student.
Delighted that she still fit into them, she also found a black T-shirt with the word Guadalajara on it, and drove out in that in the morning.
She stopped on the shoulder, got out of the car and leaned against its side.
She pinned up her hair, then let it down.
She put on her glasses, then took them off.
She rolled up her jeans, then rolled them back down.
Then she opened the back door to find a comb and tame that damned strand, when suddenly she heard a honk.
He had arrived.
— My lovely, — he said.
The strand stayed uncombed.
— Thank you, — said Miyechka.
He smelled of himself, of love, of guilt.
She had thought he smelled only of a drop of perfume, but that, of course, was not so.
— You smell of summer, — he said, smiling.
— Follow me, — said Miyechka.
It was unusual to only embrace him, and now — to drive alone with herself and only occasionally glance at his silver car in the mirror.
— Bad color, — she said when they pulled up to the gate.
— Good engine, — he retorted.
The sun had already risen above the poplars and shone gently.
These were those rare cool summer days.
— Are you the craftsman?
— the grandmother came up to the gate.
The craftsman stood with Miyechka by his ugly silver car with the good engine.
— Ustym, — he took the grandmother by the hand.
— And you, then, are Teodora Ustymivna.
— That's me, — the grandmother nodded.
— And you're named the same as my father.
They didn't even use that name back then.
How old are you?
— Twenty-seven, — answered Ustym.
He was in a gray T-shirt and jeans.
From his figure you could carve Roman statues and then set them in the Louvre.
The T-shirt fell from his broad shoulders, catching on his chest.
When he raised his arms, the line between the T-shirt and his belt showed.
He took a case of tools from the trunk.
Marta came out.
— Good afternoon, — she said, looking him up and down with a stern gaze.
— Come with me.
Marta walked on ahead.
Ustym smiled at Miyechka and followed Marta.
— Such a cultured craftsman, — said the grandmother.
— Maybe he's a believer?
Lilichka laughed.
— But no, — the grandmother went on, — he came on Ivan Kupala, after all.
Or maybe he's a Baptist, and they don't celebrate Ivan Kupala?
— Maybe, — Miyechka smiled.
Ustym took the ladder and climbed onto the roof.
While he laid out his tools on the roof, all the women came outside, including Lusya.
The little girl ran around the house and watched the man walk along the roof.
— Look!
— she shouted.
— Look!
— The mister craftsman, — said the grandmother.
— He's fixing our roof so little Lusya won't be wet when she sleeps.
— Look!
— Lusya exclaimed.
Marta had put on the grandfather's light-blue shirt, because she could barely fit into her own one with the poppies anymore.
She stood and looked at the craftsman with distrust.
— I hope his hands grow from the right place, — she said.
— Oh, just look at those shoulders, — retorted Maria.
She had just come outside with the tea.
The craftsman worked on the roof, hammering something, banging, carrying things.
He came down from the roof and climbed back up.
Miyechka stood by the jasmine bushes and looked up.
The sun was beginning to warm up, and it was getting hot in the jeans.
She wondered whether he would finally take off his T-shirt, but he seemed to pay attention neither to the sun, nor to the heat, nor to Miyechka, and simply worked.
She knew that, unlike herself, he was always able to focus on a single thing.
— Mia, are you comfortable here in the bushes?
— said the grandmother, whom Miyechka hadn't even noticed.
She was walking slowly with her cane through the tall grass.
— Solomiya began.
— Granny, I'm smelling the jasmine.
— Aha, aha, — the grandmother nodded.
— It'd be better to smell it while it was in bloom, but now will do too…
Miyechka laughed.
The two of them stood and looked up.
Ustym worked.
— A good craftsman, Mia.
Only, you see, he's not a believer.
But handsome.
Like an actor.
— Not a believer, — retorted Mia.
— And thank the Lord, — said the grandmother.
— Were he a believer, we'd be sleeping in a puddle until Monday.
— Isn't he hot?
— Lilichka spoke up.
She had come to the jasmine with a chair.
— Granny, sit down, — she said.
— He probably is hot, — said Miyechka.
— Then I'll bring a chair for myself too, — said Lilichka and went off.
Before long Marta appeared in the doorway leading out to the garden.
— Maybe someone wants tea?
— she asked from the threshold.
— The child has fallen asleep.
— Oh, you put her to sleep, — Lilichka started.
— Well, she nearly did it herself, — Marta retorted, as if justifying that she hadn't really put her to sleep all that much.
— We're busy here, — retorted the grandmother.
Marta walked over to them barefoot across the grass.
She saw that everyone was riveted to the roof, looked over to where Ustym stood against the sky, pressing some plank to his stomach.
— I see, — said Marta.
Lilichka suddenly jumped up and ran off somewhere.
A minute later she appeared with a little plastic table.
She set it down by the bushes and a moment later brought two more chairs.
— Marta, leave the door open so Lusya can be heard, and sit down, — said Lilichka.
Only Miyechka was left without a chair.
— I'll bring one for myself too, — she said and went off toward the way out of the garden, all the while turning her head toward Ustym.
— As long as she doesn't kill herself, — said the grandmother.
— At least we're sitting, — Marta laughed.
Miyechka went into the house and looked at herself once more.
She turned around by the mirror, looked at how the jeans fit her, and remained displeased with them.
It was hot, and they weighed her down.
She remembered how in the morning she'd gone through all her clothes and could find nothing that suited this meeting.
She quietly took off the jeans and the T-shirt.
She touched her breasts with her hands, her body was sticky from the heat, she imagined this whole summer, this house and garden being only for her and for him.
But she quickly remembered that, besides her, five other women lived here, and that she was, after all, glad of it.
There are houses in plenty elsewhere, but there is no other like this one, where everyone gathers.
She stood quietly before the shelves with her own things, which had long since gotten mixed up with Marta's and Lilichka's.
You could hear Lusya snuffling behind the wall in the living room.
'We have a girl,' she recalled her own words on the way back from the tire shop.
She went up to the kitchen sink, turned on the water and washed her face.
Not just her face — she also splashed water on her chest, back and belly.
— Made a mess, — she said to herself.
— It's hot, it'll dry in a moment.
Where else could she live with five people and not be afraid to walk around the kitchen naked.
Having pinned up her loose hair and put on an unremarkable blue knee-length sleeveless dress with a little opening on the back, Miyechka went outside.
Her freshly dampened hair stuck to the back of her neck.
Not finding the last garden chair, she grabbed a stool with a red checked little cushion fastened to it and went out into the garden.
In the garden Maria was sitting on the last garden chair.
Lilichka stood — on her chair sat Mrs. Darochka, the neighbor across the river.
Walk what was once a street, then the riverbank, then the little footbridge, the vegetable garden — and there's the first neighbor.
Mrs. Darochka.
— Good afternoon, — Miyechka greeted her.
— Good health to you, dear, — Mrs. Darochka called everyone she considered younger 'dear,' including the ninety-six-year-old Teodora, who was seven years older than Mrs. Darochka.
